content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 8, 1993


Greg Hill
338 The Village
Townhouse 2
Redondo Beach, California 90277

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-136

Dear Mr. Hill:

As a candidate for City Council in Redondo Beach, you have
requested advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act.¹ Specifically, you have asked whether
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act preclude you from
serving on the city council because of your employment.

You are an employee of The Gas Company and have stock
ownership in Pacific Enterprises Corporation, which is the parent
company of The Gas Company. The conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act do not preclude a public official from holding office
based on the official's employment. However, all public officials
are subject to the disqualification provisions of the Act.

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.

An official has a financial interest in a governmental deci-
sion within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
the official, on a member of the official's immediate family,
or on any of the financial interests listed in Section 87103.


________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-136
Page 2


     Therefore, if elected, you must disqualify yourself from
participating in any decision which will have a material financial
effect on The Gas Company or Pacific Enterprises Corporation.

     Enclosed is a pamphlet on the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Act.  I trust this answers your question.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Jeff Marschner
                                        General Counsel

                                        By:  Jill Stedner
                                             Counsel, Legal Division


JM:JS:dl

enc.

sections.question: As a candidate for City Council in Redondo Beach, whether the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act preclude you from serving on the city council because of your employment with The Gas Company and stock ownership in Pacific Enterprises Corporation, which is the parent company of The Gas Company.
sections.conclusion: The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not preclude a public official from holding office based on the official's employment. However, all public officials are subject to the disqualification provisions of the Act. Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest. Therefore, if elected, you must disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which will have a material financial effect on The Gas Company or Pacific Enterprises Corporation.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a candidate for city council can serve while employed by The Gas Company and holding stock in its parent company Pacific Enterprises Corporation under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Employment and stock ownership do not preclude serving on city council, but the applicant must disqualify themselves from any governmental decisions that would have a material financial effect on their employer or its parent company under Section 87100 of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. File number A-93-136, dated April 8, 1993. Letter signed by Jeff Marschner, General Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: As a candidate for City Council in Redondo Beach, whether the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act preclude you from serving on the city council because of your employment with The Gas Company and stock ownership in Pacific Enterprises Corporation, which is the parent company of The Gas Company.

CONCLUSION: The conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act do not preclude a public official from holding office based on the official's employment. However, all public officials are subject to the disqualification provisions of the Act. Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know they have a financial interest. Therefore, if elected, you must disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which will have a material financial effect on The Gas Company or Pacific Enterprises Corporation.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that employment with The Gas Company and stock ownership in its parent company Pacific Enterprises Corporation do not preclude a candidate from serving on Redondo Beach City Council, but the official must disqualify themselves from decisions with material financial effects on these entities.
